does: This paper proposes Weights-Rotated Preference Optimization (RoPO), a novel preference alignment algorithm that addresses reward hacking in Direct Preference Optimization. The core contribution identifies representation redundancy caused by neuron collapse as the underlying cause of pathological behaviors like excessive length generation and knowledge forgetting. RoPO combines implicit constraints through KL divergence on output logits (inherited from DPO) with explicit constraints on intermediate hidden states using multi-granularity orthogonal matrix fine-tuning. The method prevents the policy model from deviating excessively from the reference model, thereby preserving pre-training and supervised fine-tuning knowledge while still achieving preference alignment. Experimental results demonstrate improvements of up to 3.27 points across benchmarks, indicating the approach successfully mitigates reward hacking while maintaining model capability and diversity.
connects: This work directly builds on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and addresses known limitations in reward-based alignment of large language models. It extends prior research on preference learning and alignment techniques by diagnosing a specific mechanistic problem—neuron collapse in the parameter space—that contributes to reward hacking in existing methods. The paper draws connections to broader literature on model drift during fine-tuning, knowledge retention during adaptation, and the role of representation geometry in preventing catastrophic forgetting. It also engages with work on orthogonal constraints and weight regularization techniques used in other domains to maintain model stability. The investigation of hidden state evolution during preference optimization connects to mechanistic interpretability research examining how neural network structure changes during training.
enables: This work opens avenues for deeper investigation into the mechanistic causes of misalignment during preference optimization, particularly how neuron geometry and representation collapse relate to model behavior. Future research can build on the explicit constraint framework using orthogonal matrices to develop more sophisticated regularization techniques that preserve interpretability and knowledge. The approach suggests potential applications to other preference learning paradigms and alignment techniques beyond DPO. The diagnostic methodology for identifying representation redundancy could inform safer fine-tuning protocols and better understanding of when and why models optimize for proxy objectives. Additionally, this work may enable more reliable evaluation metrics for detecting reward hacking and developing alignment methods that scale better while maintaining the diversity and knowledge of larger models.
